text: The surgeon injects vasopressin into the uterus, causing a marble white appearance of the serosa over the intramural myoma. Approximately 100 ml of saline is injected to achieve hemostasis and hydro dissection, creating visible transient ischemia in the myoma.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make an anterior longitudinal incision over the serosal surface and uterine muscles to access the intramural myoma. As the incision progresses through the muscle layers, the pearly white capsule of the myoma becomes visible. A myoma screw is applied to provide anterior traction, facilitating the enucleation of the myoma. A suction irrigation instrument is used to articulate and separate the myoma from the surrounding muscle layers.

The surgeon uses a single myoma screw to apply traction on the myoma for easier enucleation, utilizing a four-port laparoscopic technique. Two ipsilateral ports and one contralateral port are positioned according to the baseball diamond concept to optimize manipulation angles.

The surgeon rearranges the myoma screw to continue enucleating the intramural myoma. Approximately half of the myoma is visible outside the uterine muscle, while the remaining portion is carefully manipulated to avoid excessive avulsion force. The myoma screw is repositioned to the left side, and the surgeon uses both hands to facilitate further enucleation.

The surgeon identifies a lobulated myoma, revealing a larger continuation behind the initially visible mass. The entire myoma is carefully enucleated without opening the uterine cavity, ensuring complete removal. The muscle is separated, and the surgical field remains dry with no bleeding or damage to feeding vessels. Suturing begins following the removal.

The surgeon applies different layers of suturing to obliterate the dead space using an extracorporeal knot. A 30mm endoske needle with number one vicryl is used for continuous suturing, including a dandy jamming knot and Misra's knot with an additional lock for security. A darker knot pusher is utilized to ensure the sutures are tight, and the suture is initially cut long for later trimming.

The surgeon applies tension sutures to approximate the layers of the uterine wall, using Misra's knot technique with multiple hitches, winds, and locks. The knots are secured with a Vandarkar knot pusher. The effect of vasopressin is visibly diminishing, prompting the surgeon to close the knots quickly to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon slides the western knot using the medline, pulling the tail to lock it as part of extracorporeal knotting. Multiple knots are applied to approximate the dead space, followed by continuous suturing using the dandy jamming knot technique. The assistant holds the tissue while the surgeon takes bites and locks the suture, visibly flattening the uterus. The flattened appearance is temporary, as the uterus will regain its normal shape upon healing.

During the laparoscopic myomectomy, the surgeon performs continuous suturing, meticulously taking deep bites to ensure the closure of all spaces. An assistant holds the suture to maintain tension and prevent loosening, ensuring the margins are adequately maintained.

The surgeon uses the tail of the previous extracorporeal knot to lock and terminate the continuous suturing. Multiple knots are applied to secure the closure. Thorough irrigation and suction are performed to ensure there is no bleeding, with any collected blood being removed from the posterior cul-de-sac.

The patient's head is elevated as the surgeon uses suction to remove fluid from the pelvic cavity. The myoma is positioned in the right iliac fossa, and the uterus appears clean. The pelvic area is washed out, and some fluid is left to prevent adhesions. The surgeon begins the process of morselation, which involves gradually breaking down the myoma.
